content.text: What It Is

RAG status—Red, Amber, Green—is a three-level health indicator used in programme management to communicate the current risk level of a programme component at a glance. When applied consistently through published escalation rules, RAG status transforms subjective impressions of programme health into a shared, actionable language that programme managers, team leads, and stakeholders can all interpret the same way. The escalation rules define exactly what conditions trigger each status level and what actions are required in response, removing the ambiguity that makes ad-hoc status reporting unreliable.

In agentic AI programmes, RAG status is applied across multiple dimensions simultaneously: schedule health, technical risk, model performance, dependency status, team capacity, and compliance posture. Each dimension has its own set of thresholds that determine the appropriate status level. A programme component may be green on schedule while being amber on model performance—both statuses are relevant and neither cancels the other. The overall programme RAG is the most critical status of any dimension, unless specific governance rules specify otherwise.

Escalation rules define the response protocol for amber and red statuses: who is notified, within what timeframe, with what information, and what authority to make decisions. Without explicit escalation rules, programme managers must make ad-hoc judgements about when to escalate and to whom, leading to inconsistent behaviour that either over-escalates—creating executive fatigue—or under-escalates—allowing risks to materialise without adequate leadership awareness.

The value of RAG status and escalation rules is their combination: a RAG status without escalation rules is just a colour-coding convention. Escalation rules without a shared understanding of what each status means produce inconsistent escalations. Together, they create a programme management system where health signals automatically trigger defined responses, reducing the dependence on individual programme managers to know when and how to escalate.
Why It Matters

The most common governance failure in large agentic AI programmes is that critical risks are known at the team level but never reach the stakeholders who have the authority to address them. A team lead who knows that a model accuracy target is unlikely to be met may not know whether this warrants immediate escalation or whether it can be addressed locally. Without clear escalation rules, the risk of the team lead under-escalating because they want to solve the problem themselves before raising it—commonly called 'happy path reporting'—is high.

Clear escalation rules shift the responsibility for escalation from the judgement of individual team leads to the application of published criteria. When a team lead knows that 'any dependency with a confidence level of low that affects the critical path automatically triggers a programme-level escalation,' they do not need to make a personal judgement call about whether the situation warrants attention. The rule makes the decision for them, reducing both under-escalation and the anxiety that accompanies the decision to raise a concern.

RAG status visibility at the programme level also improves the quality of governance decisions. When the programme sponsor can see at a glance which workstreams are red, which are amber, and which are green—and has trust that those assessments are accurate because they are derived from published rules rather than individual judgements—they can direct their limited attention to the highest-risk areas rather than giving equal attention to all programme components.
Benefits
Clear communication language:
A shared RAG vocabulary with published definitions enables fast, unambiguous communication of health status across different teams and seniority levels.
Consistent escalation:
Published escalation rules ensure that similar situations trigger similar responses regardless of which team or programme manager is involved.
Reduced under-escalation:
Explicit thresholds remove the psychological barrier to escalating concerns by making escalation a rule-following act rather than a personal initiative.
Management by exception:
Green items require no action; amber and red items trigger defined responses. This enables programme managers to focus attention efficiently.
Governance documentation:
RAG status histories and escalation records provide evidence of governance diligence for audits and post-programme reviews.
Predictable stakeholder communication:
Stakeholders know that red statuses will always trigger escalation and can trust that the absence of escalation means no components are red.
Trade-offs and Limitations
Threshold setting difficulty:
Choosing the right thresholds for RAG status changes requires judgement about what risk level warrants which response; poorly calibrated thresholds produce either too many or too few escalations.
False precision:
RAG status discards nuance. Two items at amber may have very different risk profiles that are not captured by the single status level.
Gaming risk:
Teams may report green to avoid escalation procedures, especially if escalation is associated with negative consequences rather than supportive intervention.
Status fatigue:
In large programmes with many components, a constant stream of amber and red statuses can overwhelm programme managers and create desensitisation.
Delayed transitions:
Teams may delay transitioning from green to amber or amber to red because they believe they can resolve the issue before anyone notices, defeating the early-warning purpose of the system.
How It Works

RAG status rules define the conditions for each status level for each dimension being tracked. For schedule: green means on track or ahead; amber means up to two weeks behind but still achievable without scope reduction; red means more than two weeks behind or requiring scope reduction to meet the delivery date. For technical risk: green means no known technical blockers; amber means a technical blocker identified with a mitigation path; red means a technical blocker with no clear resolution path. These definitions are published and agreed by all stakeholders before the programme begins.

Escalation rules specify the response protocol for each status level. Green requires no action beyond routine reporting. Amber requires the team lead to notify the programme manager within twenty-four hours with a risk assessment and proposed mitigation; the programme manager reviews and decides whether to escalate further. Red requires immediate escalation to the programme manager, who must escalate to the programme sponsor within four business hours with a situation report, impact assessment, and recommended response options.

Status assessment should be performed weekly for all tracked dimensions as part of the regular reporting cycle. Automated status assessments—where dashboards compute RAG status from metric thresholds without manual input—are more reliable than manual assessments because they apply rules consistently and are not subject to optimistic bias. Manual assessments are appropriate for dimensions that cannot be captured by quantitative thresholds, such as team morale, stakeholder sentiment, or strategic alignment.
Architecture

The escalation flow begins with assessing the current state of each tracked dimension against published thresholds. The assessment engine—automated or manual—applies the RAG rules to produce a status for each dimension. The status is notified to the dimension owner, who is responsible for managing green items routinely and initiating the escalation protocol for amber items. Red statuses trigger immediate escalation to programme management, which escalates to programme leadership within the defined timeframe. Resolution tracking closes the loop by recording the actions taken and confirming when the status has returned to amber or green.
When to Use It
All multi-team programmes:
RAG status provides a universal health signal that works across all programme types and team configurations.
Programmes with governance oversight:
Steering committees and executive sponsors benefit from RAG status as a concise programme health indicator that focuses their attention efficiently.
Programmes with regulatory requirements:
Explicit, documented escalation rules and status histories provide the audit evidence required in regulated environments.
Programmes at delivery risk:
Clear escalation thresholds ensure that delivery risks surface early enough for meaningful management intervention.
How to Implement
Define dimensions to track:
Identify the dimensions that matter for your programme—schedule, technical risk, model performance, dependencies, budget, compliance—and publish a RAG definition for each.
Set thresholds:
For each dimension, define the specific conditions that trigger green, amber, and red status. Make the thresholds as quantitative as possible for consistency.
Define escalation protocols:
For each status level, specify who is notified, within what timeframe, with what content, and who is responsible for initiating the escalation.
Publish and agree:
Share the RAG definitions and escalation rules with all team leads and stakeholders at programme kickoff, securing explicit agreement.
Train team leads:
Ensure team leads understand the rules and can apply them correctly; confusion about thresholds is a common source of inconsistent reporting.
Build or configure the assessment mechanism:
Automate status assessment where possible; for manual dimensions, provide a structured form that guides the assessor through the rules.
Review and calibrate:
After the first month of operation, review whether the thresholds are producing the right number of escalations. Adjust if the system is over- or under-escalating.
Examples

# rag_assessment.py — automated RAG status assessment for agentic AI programmes

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable, Dict, Any

@dataclass
class RAGRule:
    dimension: str
    description: str
    green_condition: Callable[[Dict[str, Any]], bool]
    amber_condition: Callable[[Dict[str, Any]], bool]
    # Red = not green and not amber

    def assess(self, data: Dict[str, Any]) -> str:
        if self.green_condition(data):
            return "green"
        elif self.amber_condition(data):
            return "amber"
        return "red"

# Define rules for each programme dimension
RULES = [
    RAGRule(
        dimension="schedule",
        description="Milestone delivery vs plan",
        green_condition=lambda d: d["days_behind"] <= 0,
        amber_condition=lambda d: d["days_behind"] <= 10,
    ),
    RAGRule(
        dimension="model_accuracy",
        description="Agent task success rate vs target",
        green_condition=lambda d: d["actual_accuracy"] >= d["target_accuracy"],
        amber_condition=lambda d: d["actual_accuracy"] >= d["target_accuracy"] * 0.9,
    ),
    RAGRule(
        dimension="dependencies",
        description="Critical dependency health",
        green_condition=lambda d: d["red_deps"] == 0 and d["amber_deps"] == 0,
        amber_condition=lambda d: d["red_deps"] == 0,
    ),
    RAGRule(
        dimension="budget",
        description="Spend vs plan",
        green_condition=lambda d: d["spend_variance_pct"] <= 5,
        amber_condition=lambda d: d["spend_variance_pct"] <= 15,
    ),
    RAGRule(
        dimension="team_capacity",
        description="Capacity vs committed workload",
        green_condition=lambda d: d["utilisation_pct"] <= 90,
        amber_condition=lambda d: d["utilisation_pct"] <= 105,
    ),
]

def assess_programme_health(programme_data: Dict[str, Dict[str, Any]]) -> Dict[str, str]:
    results = {}
    for rule in RULES:
        dim_data = programme_data.get(rule.dimension, {})
        results[rule.dimension] = rule.assess(dim_data)
    # Overall = worst status
    status_order = {"red": 2, "amber": 1, "green": 0}
    overall = max(results.values(), key=lambda s: status_order[s])
    results["overall"] = overall
    return results

if __name__ == "__main__":
    current_data = {
        "schedule": {"days_behind": 3},
        "model_accuracy": {"actual_accuracy": 0.87, "target_accuracy": 0.90},
        "dependencies": {"red_deps": 0, "amber_deps": 2},
        "budget": {"spend_variance_pct": 8},
        "team_capacity": {"utilisation_pct": 95},
    }
    health = assess_programme_health(current_data)
    for dim, status in health.items():
        print(f"  {dim}: {status.upper()}")
Real-World Example

An insurance company running an AI underwriting assistance programme experienced repeated late escalations: risks that had been known at the team level for one to three weeks reached the programme sponsor only after the window for effective intervention had closed. An investigation revealed that team leads were interpreting amber and red status subjectively and delaying escalation because they were confident they would resolve the issue before it needed senior attention.

The programme implemented explicit RAG rules with quantitative thresholds—for example, 'any model accuracy metric more than five percentage points below target is automatically amber; more than ten points is red'—and automated the status assessment for the three most critical dimensions using the evaluation dashboard API. The escalation protocol was published as a one-page reference and reviewed at every programme sync. In the two quarters following the change, the average time between risk identification and sponsor notification dropped from eleven days to less than two, and two risks that would previously have become critical incidents were resolved before they affected the delivery timeline.
Common Mistakes
Subjective thresholds:
Threshold definitions like 'significantly behind' or 'major risk' are interpreted differently by different people, producing inconsistent status assessments.
No published escalation protocol:
Team leads who do not know the escalation steps will make ad-hoc decisions that vary by individual and situation.
Penalising accurate red reports:
When amber and red statuses trigger uncomfortable scrutiny or performance consequences, teams report green until the situation is undeniable.
Ignoring amber statuses:
Amber statuses that are acknowledged but not acted upon teach teams that the escalation system has no teeth.
Single overall RAG without dimensions:
A single programme RAG hides the dimension-level variation that is most actionable for programme management.
Best Practices
Use quantitative thresholds wherever possible:
Thresholds expressed as numbers—days behind, percentage points below target, number of red dependencies—are applied consistently.
Automate status assessment:
Automated RAG assessments from real data are more reliable than manual assessments and remove the temptation to over-report green.
Celebrate accurate amber and red reports:
Explicitly recognise team leads who report bad news early as demonstrating the programme management behaviour the organisation wants to encourage.
Review escalation effectiveness quarterly:
Assess whether escalations triggered timely and appropriate responses. If not, adjust the protocol or the escalation targets.
Keep escalation protocols short:
A one-page escalation reference that team leads can consult quickly is more likely to be used than a detailed procedure document.
Track status trends:
A rolling four-week RAG trend reveals whether programme health is improving or deteriorating, providing more context than a point-in-time snapshot.
Performance and Scalability

In large programmes with many tracked dimensions and components, automated RAG assessment is essential for scalability. Manual assessment of fifty or more programme components weekly is not feasible. The assessment system should be designed to run automatically on a schedule, pulling data from connected tools, computing statuses, and generating alerts for status changes without requiring programme manager input. Programme managers review the output rather than producing it.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Jira dashboards with RAG gadgets:
Built-in RAG status displays for Jira-tracked programmes, with automatic colour coding based on configurable criteria.
Power BI or Tableau:
Business intelligence tools that can compute RAG status from programme data and display trends over time.
Custom Python scripts:
Lightweight automation for computing RAG status from API data and sending alert notifications, suitable for teams that prefer custom tooling.
Monday.com or Smartsheet:
Programme management tools with native RAG status fields and configurable automation rules.
Statuspage.io:
Public-facing status pages for programmes with external stakeholders who need visibility into service health.
Comparison with Alternatives
Narrative-only status reporting:
Provides richer context but slower to scan and harder to aggregate across many components.
Percentage-complete reporting:
More granular than RAG but harder to interpret and more susceptible to inaccurate estimation.
Traffic light without escalation rules:
Provides visual clarity but without defined response protocols, the status signal does not trigger consistent action.
RAG with escalation rules (this approach):
Balances simplicity with actionability; the combination of clear definitions and defined responses makes the system effective rather than decorative.
Security and Reliability

RAG status systems that are authoritative for programme governance decisions must be reliable. Automated assessments that compute incorrect statuses due to data quality issues can trigger unwarranted escalations or—more dangerously—fail to trigger warranted ones. Data validation checks should verify that the inputs to automated assessments are within expected ranges before computing statuses. Anomalous input values—a metric that has gone to zero because a data pipeline failed—should trigger a 'data unavailable' status rather than silently producing an incorrect RAG assessment.
Key Takeaways
RAG status with published, quantitative thresholds produces consistent health assessments that do not depend on individual judgement.
Escalation rules define who is notified, within what timeframe, and with what content when amber or red status is triggered, removing ambiguity from escalation decisions.
Automating RAG assessment from real data improves consistency and removes the temptation to over-report green.
A culture that rewards early bad news is as important as the technical system; rules without cultural support produce gaming rather than honest reporting.
Status trends over time are more informative than point-in-time snapshots; track and visualise four-to-eight-week RAG trends for each dimension.
The overall programme RAG should be the worst status of any dimension to ensure that no deteriorating dimension is hidden by good performance in others.
Designing Escalation Protocols That Get Used

An escalation protocol that looks good on paper but is never followed provides false assurance—stakeholders believe that risks are being surfaced when in fact they are not. Designing escalation protocols that team leads actually use requires understanding why escalation protocols fail and designing against those failure modes.

The most common reason escalation protocols are not followed is that team leads do not know the exact steps they are supposed to take. A protocol published in a programme handbook that was distributed at kickoff and never referenced again is not an operational protocol. Effective protocols are embedded in the team's regular workflow—referenced in every team meeting agenda, visible on the programme's Confluence home page, and revisited during programme retrospectives when escalation behaviour is reviewed.

The second most common reason is that team leads fear the consequences of escalation. If past escalations have resulted in increased scrutiny of the escalating team, demands for detailed explanations that consume working time, or implicit signals that the team is underperforming, team leads will avoid escalation even when the protocol says it is required. Programme leaders must actively counter this dynamic by making escalation a valued behaviour, responding to escalations with supportive action rather than interrogation, and publicly recognising team leads who escalate early.

The third reason is that the protocol is too complex. A multi-step escalation process with different contacts for different types of issues, different timeframes for different severity levels, and different documentation requirements for each step will be remembered imperfectly and applied inconsistently. The simplest escalation protocol that achieves the governance objective is the right one. For most agentic AI programmes, a two-level protocol—team lead to programme manager for amber, programme manager to sponsor for red—with simple notification templates is sufficient.

# escalation_notifier.py — send structured escalation notifications

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional
from datetime import datetime

@dataclass
class EscalationContext:
    dimension: str        # "schedule" | "model_accuracy" | "dependency" | etc.
    current_status: str   # "amber" | "red"
    description: str      # What is the issue?
    impact: str           # What is the business/delivery impact?
    mitigation: Optional[str]  # What is the team doing about it?
    help_needed: Optional[str]  # What does the team need from the recipient?
    escalating_team: str
    escalation_contact: str  # email or Slack handle of recipient

def format_escalation_message(ctx: EscalationContext) -> str:
    status_label = "AMBER - Action Needed" if ctx.current_status == "amber" else "RED - Immediate Escalation"
    timestamp = datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M")
    lines = [
        f"[{status_label}] Programme Escalation - {ctx.dimension}",
        f"From: {ctx.escalating_team} | Time: {timestamp}",
        "",
        f"SITUATION: {ctx.description}",
        f"IMPACT: {ctx.impact}",
    ]
    if ctx.mitigation:
        lines.append(f"MITIGATION IN PROGRESS: {ctx.mitigation}")
    if ctx.help_needed:
        lines.append(f"HELP NEEDED: {ctx.help_needed}")
    lines.append(f"
Please acknowledge within {'4 business hours' if ctx.current_status == 'red' else '24 hours'}.")
    return "
".join(lines)

# Example usage
ctx = EscalationContext(
    dimension="model_accuracy",
    current_status="amber",
    description="Intent classification accuracy dropped from 91% to 84% after Thursday's model update.",
    impact="Customer automation agent routing errors will increase by ~15%, affecting ~200 interactions/day.",
    mitigation="Rolled back to previous model version; root cause analysis underway.",
    help_needed="Approval to delay Q2 model update milestone by 5 days to allow retraining.",
    escalating_team="customer-automation",
    escalation_contact="programme-manager@example.com"
)
print(format_escalation_message(ctx))

Escalation templates—pre-formatted messages that team leads fill in with the specific details of their situation—significantly improve escalation quality and consistency. A template with fields for 'situation,' 'impact,' 'mitigation in progress,' and 'help needed' ensures that every escalation provides the information the recipient needs to respond effectively, without requiring the team lead to structure a message under pressure. Templates also reduce the emotional barrier to escalation by making it a form-filling activity rather than a difficult conversation.
Embed the protocol in daily workflow:
Reference the escalation protocol in every team meeting agenda and programme sync, not just the kickoff handbook.
Respond supportively to escalations:
Make it consistently true that escalating early results in help, not interrogation. This reputation is the foundation of a functioning escalation culture.
Provide escalation templates:
Pre-formatted templates with situation, impact, mitigation, and help-needed fields reduce friction and improve escalation quality.
Review escalation behaviour in retrospectives:
Examine whether the programme's escalation patterns match what the protocol specifies, and investigate deviations.
Simplify to the minimum necessary:
The simplest protocol that achieves governance objectives is the one that will actually be used.
